coredata.dc:title: A novel nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots-H2O2 chemiluminescence system for carcinoembryonic antigen detection using functional HRP-Au@Ag for signal amplification
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Cao, Jun-Tao
Zhang, Wen-Sheng
Wang, Hui
Ma, Shu-Hui
Liu, Yan-Ming
coredata.dc:description: A novel nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (NS-CDs)-H2O2 chemiluminescence (CL) system was developed to detect carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) by taking advantage of dual-signal amplification of functional Au@Ag nanoparticles (NPs) nanoprobes. Horseradish peroxidase (HRP) and the complementary DNA were co-immobilized onto Au@Ag NPs surface to shape the functional nanoprobes (HRP-Au@Ag-cDNA) for signal amplification. In this proposal, HRP-Au@Ag-cDNA was specifically hybridized with CEA aptamer-functionalized magnetic beads to form the double-strand hybridization nanocomposites (HRP-Au@Ag-dsDNA-MB). Upon the addition of CEA, the CEA aptamer preferred to bind with CEA instead of double-strand hybridization interaction, thus HRP-Au@Ag-dsDNA-MB was dehybridized and the HRP-Au@Ag-cDNA nanoprobe was released. The synergistic catalytic effects of HRP and Au @Ag NPs endow the nanoprobe producing a dual CL signal amplification in the NS-CDs-H2O2 CL system. The CL intensity of the developed strategy enhanced with CEA concentration increasing in the range of 0.3–80 ng mL−1. Benefiting from the synergistic effect, a detection limit as low as 94 pg mL−1 was obtained. Moreover, successful application of this CL sensing platform was achieved for the determination of CEA in human serum samples, demonstrating the promising prospect in the early tumor warning and therapeutic monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NS-CDs-H2O2 system
HRP-Au@Ag-cDNA nanoprobe
Chemiluminescence biosensing platform
Carcinoembryonic antigen

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of Au@Ag NPs, Au@Ag-cDNA and HRP-Au@Ag-cDNA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM (A) images of the NS-CDs; Inset: histograms of the particle size distribution of the NS-CDs. The UV–vis absorption spectrum (B) of the NS-CDs. Inset: photograph of the NS-CDs under irradiation of natural light (left) and UV light (right), (365 nm). The FL emission spectra (C) for NS-CDs excited at wavelengths ranging from 300 nm to 385 nm.
TEM (A) and HRTEM (B) images of Au@Ag NPs. (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of Au@Ag NPs (a), Au@Ag-cDNA (b), HRP (c) and HRP-Au@Ag-cDNA (d).
Effects of the concentration of NS-CDs (A) and the concentration of H2O2 (B), the pH (C) on the NS-CDs-H2O2 system.

The CL behaviors of NS-CDs-H2O2 system (A) with the addition of H2O (a), Au@Ag NPs (b), Au@Ag-cDNA (c) and HRP-Au@Ag-cDNA (d), respectively. The CL spectra (B) of NS-CDs-H2O2 (a) and NS-CDs-H2O2-nanoprobe (b).

(A) CL response of the CL biosensor in the presence of different concentration of CEA (a–h: 0.3, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 20, 40, 80 ng mL−1) (B) The linear plot between CL intensity and the logarithm of the CEA concentrations. The error bars represent standard deviations of measurements (n = 3).
(A) Specificity investigation of the proposed method for CEA detection. (B) The storage stability of the HRP-Au@Ag-cDNA nanoprobe.
(A) The schematic representations of the CEA recognition procedure and (B) The catalytic process of the cDNA-Au@Ag-HRP nanoprobe on the NS-CDs-H2O2 system.
Comparison of the proposed method with the reference methods for CEA detection.
Analytical results of the ECL ELISA method and the proposed CL biosensor for CEA in human serum samples.

head.ce:title: A novel nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots-H2O2 chemiluminescence system for carcinoembryonic antigen detection using functional HRP-Au@Ag for signal amplification

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute for Conservation and Utilization of Agro-bioresources in Dabie Mountains, Xinyang Normal University, Xinyang 464000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Institute for Conservation and Utilization of Agro-bioresources in Dabie Mountains

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Xinyang Central Hospital, Xinyang 464000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (NS-CDs)-H2O2 chemiluminescence (CL) system was developed to detect carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) by taking advantage of dual-signal amplification of functional Au@Ag nanoparticles (NPs) nanoprobes. Horseradish peroxidase (HRP) and the complementary DNA were co-immobilized onto Au@Ag NPs surface to shape the functional nanoprobes (HRP-Au@Ag-cDNA) for signal amplification. In this proposal, HRP-Au@Ag-cDNA was specifically hybridized with CEA aptamer-functionalized magnetic beads to form the double-strand hybridization nanocomposites (HRP-Au@Ag-dsDNA-MB). Upon the addition of CEA, the CEA aptamer preferred to bind with CEA instead of double-strand hybridization interaction, thus HRP-Au@Ag-dsDNA-MB was dehybridized and the HRP-Au@Ag-cDNA nanoprobe was released. The synergistic catalytic effects of HRP and Au @Ag NPs endow the nanoprobe producing a dual CL signal amplification in the NS-CDs-H2O2 CL system. The CL intensity of the developed strategy enhanced with CEA concentration increasing in the range of 0.3–80 ng mL−1. Benefiting from the synergistic effect, a detection limit as low as 94 pg mL−1 was obtained. Moreover, successful application of this CL sensing platform was achieved for the determination of CEA in human serum samples, demonstrating the promising prospect in the early tumor warning and therapeutic monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: The level change of some proteins, DNA, and enzyme in human body is greatly related with the occurrence and development of diseases, especially cancers [1–3]. Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) was first introduced as the tumor correlated antigen by Gold and Freedman [4] in 1965 and its application as a biomarker for cancers has been extensively recognized in the last few decades [5]. The previous studies indicated that the CEA content in colon tissue of a healthy adult were as low as 2.5–5.0 ng mL−1 [6,7], which makes the sensitive detection of CEA challenging. Up to now, many detection means toward CEA have been established, such as the commonly used enzyme linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [8], fluorescence spectroscopy (FL) [9], electrochemistry (EC) [10], surface plasmon resonance (SPR) approach [11], chromatography [12], and surface-enhanced Raman scattering technique [13]. Nevertheless, current methods are still faced with the dilemma of tedious experimental, expensive instruments, as well as low selectivity and sensitivity. Consequently, it is critical to develop simple and low-cost methods to realize CEA detection with high selectivity and sensitivity.

Chemiluminescence (CL) has been proved to be an effective and highly sensitive technique with simple equipment [14]. Currently, the chemicals containing peroxalate, lucigenin, luminol, Ce(IV), and potassium permanganate have been used as CL reagents. Although each of these CL reagents-based systems possesses many advantages in CL bioanalysis, each also has some limitations such as high cost or poisonous of reagents, low CL emission efficiency, or unsatisfied specificity. Accordingly, development of novel CL system with economic and environmentally friendly CL reagent is still urgent.

As a rising star of nanocarbon materials, carbon-dots (CDs) have amazed the researchers with a variety of fascinating advantages, including lower cost, facile preparation, outstanding photoactivity, and hypotoxicity. It is highly valued for CL usages in comparison with the conventional reagents [15]. To date, CDs-based CL systems have exhibited significant CL property during the oxidation processes, with the presence of some oxidants, e.g. KMnO4/Ce (IV) [16], H2O2-NaHSO3 [15], H2O2-HNO2 [17], ONOOH-carbonate [18], K2S2O8-triethylamine [19] and low-temperature plasma-induced CDs [20]. However, the application of CDs as luminophor is still sparse and the quantum yield (QY) of CDs is low. Interestingly, some reports have confirmed that doping CDs with heteroatoms such as nitrogen (N), boron (B), and sulfur (S) possesses practical significance for improving the performance of CDs [21,22]. Among them, the N and S co-doped CDs (NS-CDs) reported by Dong et al. [23] are one of the doped CDs with incredibly high QY of 73%. Enlightening by the intriguing properties of NS-CDs, we assume that the NS-CDs-based CL biosensing system would provide a new way for analysis of tumor markers.

Herein, a novel NS-CDs-H2O2 CL system was developed for CEA detection using HRP-Au@Ag-cDNA as dual-signal amplification probe. As shown in Scheme 1A, the designed HRP-Au@Ag-cDNA probe coexisting with CEA aptamer-functionalized magnetic beads (aptamer-MB) forms double-strand hybridization nanocomposites (HRP-Au@Ag-dsDNA-MB). After addition of the target CEA, the double-stranded aptamer/cDNA of HRP-Au@Ag-dsDNA-MB was dehybridized by the recognition of CEA to the aptamer. The HRP-Au@Ag-cDNA was then detached from aptamer-MB and transferred into NS-CDs-H2O2 system. Scheme 1B describes the catalytic process of the NS-CDs-H2O2 system by the HRP-Au@Ag-cDNA nanoprobe. The amount of released HRP-Au@Ag-cDNA nanoprobe increased with the augments of CEA concentration, resulting in an enhanced CL intensity. The superior dual catalytic performance of the HRP-Au@Ag-cDNA nanoprobe generated a fascinating CL response in the system. Under the optimum conditions, the developed platform was employed for the CEA analysis in human serums.

CEA, bovine serum albumin (BSA), human immunoglobulin G (hIgG), and human serum albumin (HSA), were purchased from Shanghai Solarbio Bioscience & Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), horseradish peroxidase (HRP), (3-aminopropyl)-triethoxysilane (APTES) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), HAuCl4·4H2O were from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). The solution of CEA and its DNA chain molecules were diluted with phosphate buffer solution (0.01 M, pH 7.4, NaH2PO4-K2HPO4, PBS). Other reagents of AR grade were directly used without further treatment. Pure water (18.2 MΩ·cm) from an ultrapure water system by Aike Water Treatment Solution Provider (China) was used to prepare all aqueous solutions. The sequences of the CEA binding aptamer (biotin-APT) and the complementary DNA are 5′-Biotin-TTT TAT ACC AGC TTA TTC AAT T-3′ (biotin-APT) and 5′-SH-(CH2)6-TTT TAA TTG AAT AAG C-3′ (SH-cDNA), respectively.

CL measurements were performed on an ultra-weak luminescence analyzer (BPCL-1-TIC, Institute of Biophysics, Chinese Academy of Science, Beijing, China). The CL and UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on the F-7000 FL and U3900H UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan), respectively. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution TEM (HRTEM) were conducted on a Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN microscope (FEI Co., Ltd., USA). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was carried out by S-4800 microscope (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a KAlpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Co., USA) and the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was carried out on a FTIR-8400S spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan).

The NS-CDs were prepared according to the literature [23] with minor improvement. In detail, the mixture of L-cysteine (1.0 g) and citric acid (2.0 g) was grounded to a uniform powder. The solid mixture obtained was then heated at 200 °C in stainless steel autoclave for 3 h. After cooling to ambient temperature naturally, the resulting syrupy product was dispersed into 20 mL pure water by ultrasonication. The solution pH was tuned to 7.0 by NaOH (1.0 M) aqueous solution. Next, the mixture solution was filtrated through 0.22 μm nitrocellulose filters and further subjected to dialysis (3500 Da, 48 h) for salt removal. Finally, the resulting bright-yellow solution was obtained as stock solution (4.63 mg mL−1).

The synthesis of Au@Ag NPs was followed as a former report [24]. In order to prepare Au@Ag-cDNA NPs, a mixture of HS-modified cDNA (10 μM, 100 μL) and Au@Ag NPs (2.0 mL) was gently shaken at ambient temperature for 24 h, followed by 10-minute centrifugation at 12,000 rpm to remove surplus reagents. After removal of the supernatant, the brown yellow Au@Ag-cDNA precipitate was washed and re-dispersed in 2.0 mL of PBS (pH 7.4, 0.01 M PBS).

To acquire HRP-Au@Ag-cDNA nanoprobe, HRP solution (1.0 mg mL−1, 50 μL) was dropped into Au@Ag-cDNA NPs (1.0 mL), followed by 5-hour incubation at 37 °C. The mixture obtained was washing twice by centrifugation (10 min at 12,000 rpm) for removal of surplus reagents. After blocked the unspecific sites with 1.0 wt% BSA, the HRP-Au@Ag-cDNA NPs nanoprobe were obtained by re-dispersed the precipitate in PBS (1.0 mL).

The Fe3O4 MBs and amino group-functionalized Fe3O4 MBs were prepared by a hydrothermal method according to the literature [25,26] with a slight modification. Detailed information about the synthesis and characterization of Fe3O4 MBs was provided in the supporting information (Fig. S1).

To obtain aptamer-MB, the carboxyl groups on biotin of biotin-aptamer were firstly activated by pipetting EDC (10 mg mL−1, 20 μL) and NHS (10 mg mL−1, 20 μL) into biotin-aptamer solution for 1 h reaction at 25 °C. The resultant solution was then added into the amino-terminated Fe3O4 MBs (2.0 mL). After incubated overnight at 37 °C under stirring, superfluous CEA aptamer was removed by successive washing of the Fe3O4 MBs conjugates with an external magnetic force. The nonspecific binding sites were blocked by 1.0 wt% BSA for 30 min. Excess of BSA was wiped off and the deposit was further dispersed in 0.01 M PBS. The aptamer-Fe3O4 MB conjugates were acquired.

Design and fabrication of target recognition-induced protocol was illustrated in Scheme 1A. Initially, 200 μL HRP-Au@Ag-cDNA probe was mixed with 200 μL aptamer-MB conjugates. Subsequently, the mixture was allowed for reaction for 5 h at 37 °C to form double-stranded hybridization HRP-Au@Ag-dsDNA-MB complexes. Then, the redundant HRP-Au@Ag-cDNA nanoprobe was removed by washing with an external magnetic force. Afterwards, a series different concentration of CEA was added into the HRP-Au@Ag-dsDNA-MB hybridization complexes, respectively. After incubated for 2 h at room temperature, a competitive-type reaction was achieved. Finally, the presence of CEA leads to the dehybridization of the double-stranded aptamer/cDNA owing to the specific recognition of CEA to aptamer. At this state, the HRP-Au@Ag-cDNA was detached from the aptamer-MB and released for CL measurements.

The TEM was used to characterize the morphologies of the NS-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1A, the NS-CDs are monodisperse nanoparticles with nearly spherical shape. Statistically, the diameter distribution was between 2.6 and 3.5 nm, with an average at 3.1 nm (Fig. 1A, inset) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of NS-CDs in Fig. 1B exhibited two typical peaks at 240 nm and 345 nm. The peak at 240 nm was derived from π → π* transition within the aromatic sp2 domains, while the one at 345 nm was generated by surface state with trapping excited state energy [27]. The aqueous NS-CDs solution exhibited a strong blue FL emission under UV lamp (365 nm) while showing slightly yellow under natural light (Inset of Fig. 1B). As depicted in Fig. 1C, the maximum FL emission of NS-CDs located at 420 nm with an excitation wavelength of 335 nm. In addition, the excitation wavelength and emission wavelength were independent of each other, which was similar to the previous reports [28]. The XPS and FIIR spectra indicate that the NS-CDs were composed of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and sulfur and possessed the groups of CH, CS, CC, CN, CN, NH, and some oxygen-containing groups (OH, CO, –COO, and CO). All these data prove the successful preparation of the NS-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of Au@Ag NPs, Au@Ag-cDNA and HRP-Au@Ag-cDNA
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of Au@Ag NPs from TEM image (Fig. 2A) is spherical and well dispersed in a diameter of about 24 nm. HRTEM image reveals that Au@Ag NPs is core-shell structure (Fig. 2B).

The nanoprobe assembly process of HRP-Au@Ag-cDNA was verified by UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 2C). The bare Au@Ag NPs exhibits a broad absorption at 410–500 nm (curve a). After coupling with cDNA, the peak of the conjugate shows a red shift and the absorption intensity decreased due to the decoration of biomolecules on the Au@Ag NPs (curve b). The bare HRP shows a typical absorption peak at about 406 nm (curve c), which is a typical indicator of the heme group. After Au@Ag-cDNA was coated with HRP, a red-shifted peak appears at about 415 nm (curve d), which could be attributed to the interparticle plasmon coupling. These results prove that the HRP-Au@Ag-cDNA nanoprobe was prepared as expected.

To screen the optimum concentration of NS-CDs in the CL system, the effects of NS-CDs concentration on CL intensity were tested. As shown in Fig. 3A, the stock NS-CDs solution with a dilution of 1:40 provides a higher CL intensity. The effect of H2O2 concentration on the CL system was also investigated. Fig. 3B depicts the CL signals increased with the increase of H2O2 concentration and reached a plateau at 8.0 × 10−3 M. Hence, NS-CDs with a dilution of 1:40 and H2O2 at a concentration of 8.0 × 10−3 M were chosen.

The CL behavior of the system in varied pH was also evaluated. As shown in Fig. 3C, the CL intensity enhanced with the pH increase in the range of 6.4–11.6. This is because the more OH– would produce in higher pH and thus result in the NS-CDs surface more negatively charged, facilitating the electron transfer from the conduction band of NS-CDs. Moreover, the well stability of O2 – species in high pH solution is benefit to the CL emission. When the pH value was over 11.6, the CL intensity decreased. This could be ascribed to that too many anions loaded on the surface of NS-CDs at higher pH solution suppressed the transfer of O2 – to the NS-CDs surface, resulting in the decline of CL intensity. Thus, the pH value of 11.6 was chosen in this system.

To study the CL effect of HRP-Au@Ag-cDNA nanoprobe on NS-CDs-H2O2 system, the CL behaviors of NS-CDs-H2O2 system with different additives were tested, respectively. As displayed in Fig. 4A, the Au@Ag NPs can promote the CL intensity, suggesting its well catalytic effect on the NS-CDs-H2O2 system (curve b). After immobilization of cDNA on Au@Ag NPs, the CL intensity decreased owing to slightly declined catalytic activity of Au@Ag NPs with cDNA modification (curve c). As HRP was conjugated with Au@Ag-cDNA, the HRP-Au@Ag-cDNA nanoprobe gave a strong CL response on the NS-CDs-H2O2 system (curve d). The significantly enhanced CL response could be attributed to the synergistic catalysis of Au@Ag NPs and HRP.

The CL mechanism of the system was explored by recording the CL spectra of NS-CDs-H2O2 and NS-CDs-H2O2-HRP-Au@Ag-cDNA systems using a FL spectrophotometer with the light source taken off. The CL spectra in Fig. 4B showed that both the CL peaks of NS-CDs-H2O2 and NS-CDs-H2O2-HRP-Au@Ag-cDNA systems were centered at around 500 nm with a range of 400–600 nm, which attributed to the CL emission of the excited-state NS-CDs (NS-CDs*) by the oxidant-injected holes into the NS-CDs [29]. Therefore, it is suggested that the main CL emission species comes from NS-CDs* instead of 1O2 in these CL system because the CL spectrum of 1O2 centered about 634 nm [30,31]. Moreover, compared with NS-CDs-H2O2 system, a dramatic enhancement of the NS-CDs-H2O2-HRP-Au@Ag-cDNA system was observed (Fig. 4B, curve a and b). The possible CL mechanism of the NS-CDs-H2O2-HRP-Au@Ag-cDNA system can be demonstrated in Scheme 1B and Eqs. (1)–(10) [30,32–35].

First, the H2O2 can oxidize the HRP enzyme from HRP-Au@Ag-cDNA into HRP(Fe4+ = O)Porp + (HRPI), which includes oxy-ferryl heme (Fe4+ = O) species and a porpphyrin cation radical (reaction 1). The porphyrin cation radical in HRP-I accepts one electron from negatively charged surface of NS-CDs (NS-CDs−) to generate HRP(Fe4+ = O)Porp (HRP-II) retaining heme in the ferryl (Fe4+ = O) state (reaction 2). Afterwards, HRP is regenerated by the electron transfer from NS-CDs− to HRP-II (reaction 3). Meanwhile, the Au@Ag NPs derived from HRP-Au@Ag-cDNA could catalyze H2O2 to generate HO2 −, O2 −, and H2O under the alkaline condition (reaction 4). The generated O2 – could not only react with H2O2 to produce OH, OH− , and O2 (reaction 5), but also interacted with NS-CDs to produce NS-CDs − and O2 (reaction 6). The O2 from reactions 5 and 6 can further react with OH– to produce H2O and O2 – (reaction 7). The OH acted as hole injector converts NS-CDs to NS-CDs + (reaction 8). Impressively, the electron-hole annihilation in or between the NS-CDs − and NS-CDs + formed an excited state NS-CDs (NS-CDs*) (reaction 9). In the end, the CL emission occurred when the NS-CDs* molecules return to the ground state (reaction 10).

O 2

˙ OH + NS − CDs → NS − CDs ˙

(9)

−

+
→ NS − CDs ∗ + NS − CDs
(10)
NS − CDs ∗ → NS − CDs + hν

The analytical performance of the designed method was evaluated by examining the CL responses with varied CEA concentrations. As depicted in Fig. 5A, a gradually CL increment was observed as the CEA concentration increased. The linear relationship between the CL intensity and the logarithm of the CEA concentrations from 0.3 to 80 ng mL−1 in Fig. 5B could be described with the equation of I (a.u.) = 1968.70 + 1354.02 log C CEA (ng mL−1) (R2 = 0.998). The limit of detection for CEA was 94 pg mL−1. The performance of some recent reported works was listed in Table 1 and the proposed method holds a low detection limit with relatively wide linear range.

The selectivity of the method was evaluated using two potential interfering protein HSA (40 mg mL−1) and hIgG (10 mg mL−1). As shown in Fig. 6A, two proteins gave the similar CL responses to that of the blank solution. In addition, the CEA (1.0 ng mL−1) and the mixture containing HAS (40 mg mL−1), hIgG (10 mg mL−1), and CEA (1.0 ng mL−1) also yielded the CL signals with no obvious changes.

To investigate the stability of the system, the CL response of the system for 1.0 ng mL−1 CEA was recorded with different storage time (0, 1, 2, and 3 weeks) of HRP-Au@Ag-cDNA nanoprobe. As can be seen in Fig. 6B, the CL intensity shows negligible difference within 3 weeks, indicating the acceptable stability of the system.

The precision of the proposed CL aptasensor for CEA detection were tested with intra- and inter-assay precision. The intra- and inter-assay precisions of the aptasensor were estimated by assaying 1.0 ng mL−1 CEA for six parallel measurements in one day and in three days, respectively. The relative standard deviations (RSDs) obtained for intra-assay was no more than 5.3%, while the RSD for inter-assay was less than 8.7%.

The applicability of the method was verified by determination of the CEA content in human serum samples of three healthy people (sample 1–3) and two patients (sample 4 and 5) provided by Xinyang Central Hospital. The contents of CEA (samples 4 and 5) were outside the linear range and these samples were properly diluted before the test. The proposed method was compared with the ECL ELISA as the reference (Table 2 ). The relative errors between the reference method and the proposed method are lower than 7.3%, and the RSDs are no more than 8.1%. The recovery were carried out by adding given amount of CEA (1.0 ng mL−1 and 5.0 ng mL−1 for sample 3, 10.0 ng mL−1and 50.0 ng mL−1 for sample 5) into sample 3 and 5 of the human serum, respectively. As listed in Table S1, the recoveries vary from 89.4% to 114.0%, and the RSDs are no more than 9.2%.

In this study, an innovative CL sensing strategy for CEA detection was constructed based on NS-CDs-H2O2 system with HRP-Au@Ag-cDNA nanoprobe for dual-signal amplification. The NS-CDs as CL emitter was investigated and an advanced NS-CDs-H2O2 system for bioanalysis was put forward. HRP-Au@Ag-cDNA with the features of the prominent catalytic property and good biocompatibility was selected as signal amplification tag. Combining the advantages of NS-CDs and HRP-Au@Ag-cDNA in one system, the well-designed CL biosensor enables the CEA detection with good stability, high selectivity and sensitivity. Meanwhile, the applicability of present biosensor was witnessed in the CEA analysis in human serum samples. Hence, it is expected the established CL sensing platform would have promising application in the field of biomarker detection.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21675136 and 21874115), Henan University of Science and Technology (18HASTIT003), and Nanhu Scholars Program for Young Scholars of XYNU.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2019.04.063.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Immuno-electrochemiluminescent imaging of a single cell based on functional nanoprobes of heterogeneous Ru(bpy)3 2+@ SiO2/Au nanoparticles
